pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Late-Onset Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) Deficiency (A Urea Cycle Disorder) THE SMOKING GUN: The triad of severe, unexplained Respiratory Alkalosis (due to central neurotoxicity), acute neurological decline (coma/seizures), and extremely low nitrogen waste products (BUN 3 mg/dL) indicated a severe failure of the urea cycle. The trigger—often a mild catabolic stressor like the Recent Viral Illness—pushed the patient into crisis. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: OTC is an enzyme critical for the urea cycle, converting ornithine and carbamoyl phosphate into citrulline. OTC deficiency, an X-linked disorder, prevents the body from adequately converting neurotoxic ammonia (NH3) into non-toxic urea for excretion. While severe neonatal forms are immediately obvious, late-onset forms often present in young adulthood following a catabolic stress (fasting, high protein load, fever, or viral illness). The resulting extreme hyperammonemia directly poisons the CNS, leading to mitochondrial dysfunction, NMDA receptor overstimulation, and severe astrocytic swelling, manifesting as refractory seizures and lethal cerebral edema.
